concept_name: Why Electric Current Is Not a Vector
source_book: First-principles authoring (charge conservation + vector algebra). DC Pandey Vol.1 Ch.5 used for syllabus scope only.

aha_moment.statement: Current adds like a number, not like a vector, the angle never enters.

real_world_anchor.primary: A home electrical switchboard where two supply wires merge into the main switch. The total current arriving at the switch is the arithmetic sum of the two wire currents — it does not depend on the angle at which the wires approach the box. Every electrician in India knows this in their hands; few realize it is the test that proves current is a scalar.
real_world_anchor.secondary: An auto-rickshaw stand on Brigade Road, Bengaluru. Three rickshaws arrive from three streets at random angles. The total count at the stand is one plus one plus one equals three. The angles do not enter the count. Same scalar-addition logic as currents at a junction.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A river delta near Vijayawada where the Krishna splits into branches that rejoin downstream. Total water flow downstream equals the sum of upstream branches, regardless of branch angle.

constraints: Charge is conserved at every junction (Kirchhoff's current law)
i_actual = i1 + i2 always, regardless of wire angle
i_vector_pred equals i_actual ONLY when theta = 0; for every other angle they diverge
At theta = 180 the vector model predicts |i1 - i2|, but reality still measures i1 + i2
Current density J is a vector; current i is the area-integral of J dot dA, which collapses J to a scalar
Vector classification requires BOTH magnitude+direction AND parallelogram-law obedience

STATE_1.title: Current Has Direction. So Is It a Vector?

misconception_watch.belief: If a quantity has direction, it must be a vector.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Direction is condition one of two. The next states show the second condition that current fails.

scene_composition.text: junction O

Current flows FROM -> TO.
It has a direction.

So - is it a vector
like velocity or force?

tts_sentences.text_en: Two wires meet at a junction at point O, each carrying a current.
i one is three amperes flowing in. i two is four amperes flowing in.
Current clearly has a direction, it flows from one point to another.
By that logic, shouldn't current be a vector, just like velocity or force?
Most students answer yes. The next states show exactly why that answer is wrong.

STATE_2.title: Vary the Angle. Reality Stays. Vector Prediction Wanders.

misconception_watch.belief: Vector formula must be approximately right. Physics formulas always have small errors.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: These two are exact. Reality is exactly seven amperes. Vector prediction is exactly six point zero eight at sixty degrees.

scene_composition.text_expr:
REALITY (ammeter)
i = i1 + i2 = {(i1+i2).toFixed(2)} A

Constant for every theta.
theta = {theta_deg.toFixed(0)} deg

IF current were a vector
|i| = sqrt(i1^2 + i2^2 + 2 i1 i2 cos theta)
     = {sqrt(i1*i1 + i2*i2 + 2*i1*i2*cos(theta_deg*PI/180)).toFixed(2)} A

GAP from reality
    = {abs((i1+i2) - sqrt(i1*i1 + i2*i2 + 2*i1*i2*cos(theta_deg*PI/180))).toFixed(2)} A

scene_composition.text:
AHA: Current adds like a number,
not like a vector.
The angle never enters.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch what happens when we change the angle between the two wires.
Predict, what will the ammeter read at sixty degrees?
Reality always reads seven amperes. The angle does not enter.
Current adds like a number, not like a vector. That is the rule.

STATE_3.title: Kirchhoff's Junction Rule, The Formal Proof

misconception_watch.belief: Charge conservation has nothing to do with whether current is a vector.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Charge conservation forces algebraic addition with no angle term. That is exactly what disqualifies current.

scene_composition.text:
Kirchhoff's junction rule
(charge conservation)

Charge in = Charge out
=> i1 + i2 = i

NO angle term.
NO cosine.
Pure scalar arithmetic.

tts_sentences.text_en: Why must this be true? Charge cannot pile up at a junction.
Predict, three amperes in here, four in there, what flows out?
Seven amperes, the only answer that conserves charge.
Kirchhoff's rule. No angle term. Pure scalar arithmetic.

STATE_4.title: The Two Conditions for a Vector

misconception_watch.belief: All scalar quantities are just plain numbers without any direction-feel.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Some scalars carry direction-feel. Pressure, surface tension, current. They still fail the parallelogram-law test.

scene_composition.text:
Vector requires BOTH

(1) magnitude AND direction
(2) parallelogram law

Current satisfies (1).
Current fails (2).

=> Current is a scalar.
(1) magnitude + direction  <- current passes
(2) parallelogram law  <- current FAILS

Same trap class

* pressure
  (F/A, magnitude only)
* surface tension
  (boundary scalar)
* finite rotation
  (axis exists, fails
   commutativity)

All fail condition (2).

tts_sentences.text_en: A real vector needs two things, not one.
Magnitude and direction is condition one. Current passes this.
What is condition two? Predict before reading on.
Obey the parallelogram law. Current fails. So do pressure, surface tension, finite rotation.

state_5_quantity_cards.label: Current
state_5_quantity_cards.trap_hint: has direction-of-flow but fails parallelogram law

state_5_quantity_cards.label: Surface tension
state_5_quantity_cards.trap_hint: has pull direction but obeys scalar arithmetic

scene_composition.text:
Tap each quantity as Scalar or Vector.
Use the two-condition rule.
Direction-feel is not enough.

[ Card carousel renders here ]

Current  Displacement  Pressure
Force  Surface tension  Velocity
[ Scalar ]      [ Vector ]

Score: 0 / 6

The two-condition rule:
(1) magnitude + direction
(2) obeys parallelogram law

tts_sentences.text_en: Six quantities. Tap each one as scalar or vector.
Apply the two-condition rule. Direction-feel alone is not enough.
Three of these are the canonical traps you just learned to dismantle.
Tap your way through. The score panel shows your reasoning live.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Anything with a direction is a vector.
trigger_phrases: current has direction so it is a vector
anything with direction is a vector
current must be a vector it flows one way
why is current not a vector even though it has direction
if it has direction its a vector right

scene_composition.text:
Wrong belief

Current has direction.
Vectors have direction.
=> current is a vector.

We will test this in 3 states.

tts_sentences.text_en: Your reasoning is natural. Current flows from one end of a wire to the other.
And everything with direction, velocity, force, displacement, is a vector.
The logic looks airtight. But there is one gap, condition two.
Let me show the test that separates true vectors from scalars with direction.

STATE_2.title: What Real Vectors Do, The Parallelogram Law

scene_composition.text:
Parallelogram law
|R|^2 = |A|^2 + |B|^2 + 2|A||B| cos theta

At 90 deg: cos = 0
=> R = sqrt(9 + 16) = 5

Displacement obeys this.
Does current?

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is a real vector at work. Walk three metres east, then four metres north.
Your final displacement is not seven metres. It is five metres at fifty-three degrees.
Displacement obeys the parallelogram law. The angle between the legs matters.
That is condition two. Real vectors add by parallelogram law. Now we test current.

scene_composition.text:
Vector prediction (wrong)
|i| = sqrt(9 + 16 + 24 cos 60)
    = sqrt(37) ~ 6.08 A

What the ammeter reads
i = i1 + i2
  = 3 + 4
  = 7 A  (always)

tts_sentences.text_en: Apply the parallelogram law to currents at sixty degrees.
The vector model predicts six point zero eight amperes.
Put an ammeter on the outgoing wire. You read seven amperes. Always.
Measurement wins. The vector model is wrong for current.

scene_composition.text:
Vector requires BOTH

(1)  Has magnitude AND direction
(2)  Obeys parallelogram law of addition

Current satisfies (1).
Current fails (2).

=> Current is a scalar.

Other traps that fool
your original rule

pressure         (scalar)
surface tension  (scalar)
finite rotation  (not vector)

All have direction.
All fail condition (2).

Next time you see a quantity
with direction, do not stop
at condition one. Test (2).

tts_sentences.text_en: A vector needs both conditions, not just the first.
Magnitude and direction. Obey the parallelogram law of addition.
Current passes one and fails two. So it is a scalar.
The same trap catches pressure, surface tension, finite rotation. Always test condition two.

epic_c_branches.misconception: When two currents meet at angle theta, total = sqrt(i1^2 + i2^2 + 2 i1 i2 cos theta).
trigger_phrases: use parallelogram for currents
currents at angle add by cosine rule
i = sqrt(i1 squared + i2 squared)
find resultant current
apply pythagoras to currents at junction

STATE_1.title: Wrong Belief: Pythagoras Works at Junctions

scene_composition.text:
JEE Mains question

Two wires bring 3 A and 4 A into a junction
at 60 degrees. Find total current.

Wrong belief

i = sqrt(i1^2 + i2^2 + 2 i1 i2 cos theta)
  = sqrt(9 + 16 + 12)
  = sqrt(37)
  ~ 6.08 A

Applied parallelogram law to currents.

Common JEE Mains error.
Mechanically copying the
vector addition formula.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is a JEE Mains stem you will see in mock tests.
Two currents at sixty degrees. Most students reach for the parallelogram law.
They write square root of i one squared plus i two squared plus the cosine term.
It feels right. It is the formula they just learned for vector addition.
But the answer six point zero eight is wrong. Reality is seven.

scene_composition.text:
Ammeter on outgoing wire

Reads 7.00 A
at every angle theta.

Not 6.08 A.
Not 5.00 A at 90 deg.
Always 7.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now place an actual ammeter on the outgoing wire.
At every angle, every time, the ammeter reads exactly seven amperes.
The vector formula prediction does not match the measurement.
Measurement always wins over formula. The formula was the wrong tool.

scene_composition.text:
Kirchhoff's law is not optional

Charge cannot pile up.
Charge cannot vanish.

Whatever flows in must flow out.
=> i = i1 + i2 (no angle)

The parallelogram formula
has no physical reason
to apply here.

Parallelogram law applies
to displacements, forces,
velocities, fields.

Not to current, not to mass
flow rates, not to counts.
Right tool, right job.

tts_sentences.text_en: Why does Kirchhoff's rule win and the parallelogram law fail?
Charge conservation is a law of nature. It forces algebraic addition.
The parallelogram law applies to vectors. Current is not one.
Right tool for the right job. Currents at junctions add by Kirchhoff, not by cosine.

scene_composition.text:
Currents at a junction

Step 1: identify direction (in or out)
Step 2: sum incoming
Step 3: sum outgoing
Step 4: incoming = outgoing

No angle ever enters.
No cosine, no square root.

Example

i1 = 3 A in, i2 = 4 A in
theta = 60 (irrelevant)
i_out = 3 + 4 = 7 A.

Same answer at theta = 0,
90, 120, 180.
JEE-safe procedure.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the JEE-safe procedure for currents at any junction.
Identify directions, sum incoming, sum outgoing, equate them.
No angle, no cosine, no square root. Just arithmetic.
If you ever reach for the parallelogram formula at a junction, you are wrong.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Current density J is a vector, so current i must also be a vector.
trigger_phrases: current density is vector so current is vector
integral of J dot dA
why is i scalar if J is vector
flux versus current
if J is vector then i is vector

STATE_1.title: Wrong Belief: J Vector Implies i Vector

scene_composition.text:
Wrong belief

J is a vector field.
i = integral of J dot dA.
=> i must inherit
   the vector nature of J.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the JEE Advanced level confusion. Current density J is a vector field.
And we define current as the integral of J dot dA over the cross-section.
If the input J is a vector, surely the output i is also a vector? It feels right.
It is wrong. The dot product changes everything. Watch.

STATE_2.title: The Dot Product Collapses J to a Scalar

scene_composition.text:
J . dA = |J| |dA| cos theta

Vector . Vector = SCALAR.

dot product yields a number,
not a vector.

Each contribution
to i is a scalar.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the dot product. J is a vector. dA is a vector.
But J dot dA is a scalar. The cosine theta turns it into a number.
Each tiny patch of the cross-section contributes a scalar to the total.
The dot product is where the vector nature of J disappears.

scene_composition.text:
i = integral over A of (J . dA)

Each J . dA: scalar.
Sum of scalars: scalar.

i is a scalar.
It has UNITS of amperes.
Not of amperes-with-direction.

i is the FLUX of J
through cross-section.

Flux of any vector
field is always scalar.

(Same as electric flux,
magnetic flux.)

J:  vector (A/m^2)
i:  scalar (A)

tts_sentences.text_en: We sum the dot products over the entire cross-section.
Sum of scalars is a scalar. Current i is a scalar with units amperes.
i is the flux of J through the cross-section. Flux is always scalar.
Same as electric flux, magnetic flux. Vector field, scalar flux.

scene_composition.text:
Quantity     |  Type     |  Units
-------------+-----------+--------
J            |  vector   |  A/m^2
i            |  scalar   |  A
E field      |  vector   |  V/m
electric flux|  scalar   |  V*m
B field      |  vector   |  T
magnetic flux|  scalar   |  Wb

Pattern

Field: vector.
Flux of field: scalar.

The surface integral
does the collapsing.

Remember the table.
JEE Adv tests this pair.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the pattern, written cleanly. Field versus flux.
J, electric field, magnetic field, all vectors.
Current, electric flux, magnetic flux, all scalars.
The surface integral collapses the vector. JEE Advanced tests this pair.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Scalars are just numbers without any direction-feel, so anything with direction must be a vector.
trigger_phrases: scalars cannot have direction
if it has direction it is vector
pressure has direction so it is vector
surface tension is vector
anything pointing somewhere is a vector

scene_composition.text:
Current

flows along wire
(direction-feel)

Pressure

points outward at
every surface
(direction-feel)

Surface tension

pulls along boundary
(direction-feel)

Wrong belief: direction-feel implies vector.
Three counterexamples on this canvas.
All three are scalars.

tts_sentences.text_en: If you memorized scalars are just numbers, you will fail the next three traps.
Current flows along a wire. That is direction-feel.
Pressure pushes outward at every point. Direction-feel.
Surface tension pulls along the boundary. Direction-feel.
All three are scalars. Direction-feel is not the same as being a vector.

scene_composition.text:
Pressure P = F / A

F: force (vector, has direction)
A: area (scalar magnitude)

P: force-per-unit-area
   magnitude only
   no direction in formula

The surface element vector dA
carries the direction.
Not P itself.

Air pressure at sea level
= 1.01 x 10^5 Pa

No direction component.
Just a number.

Direction-feel is a property
of the surface vector dA,
not of P.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is pressure. P equals force divided by area.
Force is a vector. Area is a magnitude. Their ratio is force-per-unit-area.
Pressure is just a number with units pascal. No direction in the formula.
The surface element vector dA carries the direction-feel, not P itself.

STATE_3.title: Surface Tension: Pulls With Direction But Adds Algebraically

scene_composition.text:
Surface tension T

T = force per unit length
  along boundary

SI unit: N / m

A scalar (magnitude only).
The pull direction is
set by the boundary geometry,
not by T itself.

Needle floating on water

Surface tension pulls
upward along the contact
line. The MAGNITUDE T
is a scalar; the upward
pull comes from boundary
orientation.

T adds algebraically.
No cosine. No vector law.

tts_sentences.text_en: Surface tension T is force per unit length, in newtons per metre.
When a needle floats, surface tension pulls along the contact line.
Direction-feel comes from boundary geometry. T itself is just a magnitude.
Two surface tensions on the same boundary add algebraically. No cosine, no vector law.

scene_composition.text:
Three classes

A. plain scalar    (mass, time, temperature)
B. scalar with     (current, pressure,
   direction-feel    surface tension)
C. vector          (force, velocity, displacement)

Class B is the trap.
Must pass parallelogram
test to escape into C.

When you meet a new
quantity, ask:
(1) does it have magnitude?
(2) does it have direction?
(3) does it obey
    parallelogram law?

All three: vector.
First two only: class B scalar.
Three classes. One test.

tts_sentences.text_en: Three classes of quantities. Plain scalar, scalar-with-direction-feel, vector.
Mass and time are class A. Current, pressure, surface tension are class B.
Force and velocity are class C, true vectors.
Class B is the trap. The parallelogram-law test is the only way to escape it.

regeneration_variants.label: Different currents, same junction logic
regeneration_variants.physical_world: i1 = 5 A, i2 = 12 A, any angle. Measured total always 17 A. Vector prediction drops from 17 (parallel) to 7 (anti-parallel).
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Kirchhoff junction rule, i = i1 + i2, angle-independent.

regeneration_variants.label: Three-wire junction (harder)
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Three wires bringing i1, i2, i3 into a junction. Measured outgoing current is i1 + i2 + i3 regardless of the three angles. Same scalar-addition behaviour, now in 3-way form.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Kirchhoff junction rule scales to any number of incoming wires, always scalar sum.

STATE_1.text: Q. Why is electric current a scalar quantity?
Statement: Current carries direction along wire but classifies as scalar.

STATE_2.text: Setup: i1 = 3 A, i2 = 4 A enter junction at angle theta.
Place ammeter on outgoing wire. Measure i_total.

STATE_4.text: Current's law: i = i1 + i2 (no cos theta)

STATE_5.text: Conclusion: current has direction-of-flow but obeys scalar arithmetic.
Therefore current is a scalar quantity.

shortcuts: Two-condition memory rule: vector iff (mag + direction) AND (parallelogram law). One-line filter for any new quantity.
JEE pattern, assertion-reason on current: A is FALSE, R is TRUE. Pick option (A is false, R is true).
Class of traps to memorize: pressure, surface tension, finite rotation, current. All have direction-feel, all fail condition (2).
edge_cases: Current density J is a vector. Current i is the area-integral i = integral of J dot dA, which collapses J to a scalar. JEE Advanced 2017 tested this pair.
Pressure has direction-of-action at any point but is defined as F/A, magnitude only. The surface element vector dA carries the direction.

Finite rotation has axis (direction) and magnitude (angle) but does NOT commute under composition, so it fails parallelogram law. Infinitesimal rotations DO commute and ARE vectors. IIT Advanced footnote.
